records.ety: [OE. waven, AS. wafian to waver, to hesitate, to wonder; akin to wæfre wavering, restless, MHG. wabern to be in motion, Icel. vafra to hover about; cf. Icel. vāfa to vibrate. Cf. Waft, Waver.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Waved (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Waving.]

defs: To play loosely; to move like a wave, one way and the other; to float; to flutter; to undulate.
quotes: His purple robes waved careless to the winds.
Where the flags of three nations has successively waved.

defs: To be moved to and fro as a signal.
To fluctuate; to waver; to be in an unsettled state; to vacillate.
quotes: He waved indifferently 'twixt doing them neither good nor harm.

defs: To move one way and the other; to brandish.
To raise into inequalities of surface; to give an undulating form a surface to.
quotes: Horns whelked and waved like the enridged sea.

defs: To move like a wave, or by floating; to waft.
To call attention to, or give a direction or command to, by a waving motion, as of the hand; to signify by waving; to beckon; to signal; to indicate.
quotes: Look, with what courteous action It waves you to a more removed ground.
She spoke, and bowing waved Dismissal.

records.ety: [From Wave, v.; not the same word as OE. wawe, waghe, a wave, which is akin to E. wag to move. √138. See Wave, v. i.]

defs: An advancing ridge or swell on the surface of a liquid, as of the sea, resulting from the oscillatory motion of the particles composing it when disturbed by any force their position of rest; an undulation.
quotes: The wave behind impels the wave before.

defs: A vibration propagated from particle to particle through a body or elastic medium, as in the transmission of sound; an assemblage of vibrating molecules in all phases of a vibration, with no phase repeated; a wave of vibration; an undulation. See Undulation.

quotes: Build a ship to save thee from the flood, I 'll furnish thee with fresh wave, bread, and wine.

defs: A waving or undulating motion; a signal made with the hand, a flag, etc.
The undulating line or streak of luster on cloth watered, or calendered, or on damask steel.

Something resembling or likened to a water wave, as in rising unusually high, in being of unusual extent, or in progressive motion; a swelling or excitement, as of feeling or energy; a tide; flood; period of intensity, usual activity, or the like; as, a wave of enthusiasm; waves of applause.
